response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "As a 39-year-old cleaner living in New York with two kids and a part-time job, I've got to be practical about social media. I only spend about an hour a day on it, mostly to unwind after work or share cooking and gardening tips with friends. Fake content bothers me because I see how it can confuse people, especially when it comes to important things like health advice or community news. With my high conscientiousness, I take responsibility for what I share. Before I post or forward anything, I pause and ask myself: Does this make sense? Does it come from a reliable source? I remember once seeing a post about a local park closing that turned out to be completely false—it caused unnecessary worry for families like mine who enjoy hiking there. My creative side helps me spot oddities in posts, like weird photos or exaggerated claims. Being Muslim, I value truthfulness, which makes me extra cautious. I don't engage much publicly due to my low extraversion, but I do talk privately with my wife or close friends if I see something suspicious. My approach is simple: verify with trusted news sites or community leaders, avoid sharing sensational stuff, and focus on positive, real connections. Social media should bring people together, not spread lies that harm our neighborhoods.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 39-year-old cleaner living in New York with two kids and a part-time job, I've got to be practical about social media. I only spend about an hour a day on it, mostly to unwind after work or share cooking and gardening tips with friends. Fake content bothers me because I see how it can confuse people, especially when it comes to important things like health advice or community news. With my high conscientiousness, I take responsibility for what I share. Before I post or forward anything, I pause and ask myself: Does this make sense? Does it come from a reliable source? I remember once seeing a post about a local park closing that turned out to be completely false—it caused unnecessary worry for families like mine who enjoy hiking there. My creative side helps me spot oddities in posts, like weird photos or exaggerated claims. Being Muslim, I value truthfulness, which makes me extra cautious. I don't engage much publicly due to my low extraversion, but I do talk privately with my wife or close friends if I see something suspicious. My approach is simple: verify with trusted news sites or community leaders, avoid sharing sensational stuff, and focus on positive, real connections. Social media should bring people together, not spread lies that harm our neighborhoods.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: Age 39 and occupation as cleaner suggest practical, grounded perspective; high conscientiousness (score 95) implies responsibility in content sharing; low extraversion (score 25) indicates preference for private verification over public debate; high agreeableness (score 70) and moderate neuroticism (score 63) lead to cautious, community-focused approach; religious beliefs (Islam) emphasize truthfulness; time on social media (one hour daily) and hobbies (cooking, gardening) inform examples about sharing practical tips; creative psychological feature aids in spotting fake content; marital status and children (married, 2 kids) highlight family concerns; education level (upper secondary) supports basic critical thinking; city of living (New York) provides context for local examples.
response_parsed.tone: Practical, cautious, community-oriented, grounded, with a focus on personal responsibility and family values.
